message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-020: make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed (stranded-resume bricking)
File: src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:145
Description: In the gate node resume loop, once apply_gate_decision validates a resume value the node calls recorder(rec) (gatenode.py:145) inside the still-open while True loop, then returns. interrupt() (:137) has already consumed and persisted the operator's resume value into the checkpoint before validation. If recorder raises (trail clock raises, payload unserializable, provenance lock contended — all real ProvenanceError modes) the exception propagates out of node(). The module's own rationale (docstring 16-21, inline 133-135) is that a raise after the resume is consumed strands the value in the checkpoint and replays it forever, which is why a BAD resume re-fires interrupt instead of raising. A recorder failure on a GOOD resume hits exactly that hazard: the validated decision is lost, and on replay the node re-fires interrupt (already_decided False, recorder never wrote the terminal record), re-prompting the operator while the prior valid resume index is gone.
PoC: def flaky_recorder(rec): raise RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable'). make_gate_node(gate, propose=..., recorder=flaky_recorder); operator submits a valid 'approved' resume; recorder raises -> exception escapes node(); the approved resume was already persisted by interrupt() and is lost on replay.
Suggested fix: Make the in-loop recorder call resilient like the rest of the protocol: either attempt-record-then-return normalizing a recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning (mirroring pipeline.py:738-747 'never mask / never strand'). Do not let a recorder exception escape after interrupt() has consumed the resume value.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate this finding by reading the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Human-in-the-loop gate node — the interrupt/resume protocol (GOVERNANCE.md).
2	
3	:func:`make_gate_node` turns an engine :class:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gates.Gate` plus a
4	proposal builder into a LangGraph node that pauses the run at the gate (``interrupt``),
5	surfaces a proposal to the operator, and on resume validates and records the decision.
6	
7	The **gate-correlation guard** rejects a resumed decision aimed at a *different* gate than the
8	one currently parked — a stale or misrouted decision (e.g. delivered late through the
9	governance channel) must not be applied to whatever gate happens to be waiting. The guard's
10	expected gate is the gate the operator named in the resume value, compared against this node's
11	gate; auto-deriving it from the parked gate would make the guard always match and thus a no-op.
12	
13	Two further hardened behaviors, ported faithfully from the prior platform's gate node:
14	
15	- **Re-prompt on a bad resume (not raise).** ``interrupt()`` consumes and *persists* the
16	  resume value into the checkpoint *before* the node returns, so raising on a malformed or
17	  mis-correlated value would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever — the gate
18	  would be permanently bricked. Instead the node validates inside a loop and re-fires
19	  ``interrupt()`` (carrying the error) on a bad value, so the operator can re-submit and
20	  actually recover.
21	- **Terminal-decision idempotency.** An ``approved`` decision is terminal: re-entering an
22	  already-approved gate is a no-op (it does not re-prompt or re-record). ``rejected`` and
23	  ``deferred`` are deliberately NOT terminal, so a reject→revise→re-review loop re-fires the
24	  interrupt as intended.
25	
26	The node returns a partial state update appending one record to ``gate_decisions``; a study's
27	graph state should declare ``gate_decisions`` with a list/add reducer so records accumulate
28	across gates. The decision/recording validator (:func:`apply_gate_decision`) is pure and
29	tested offline; only the ``interrupt`` call requires LangGraph (the optional ``orchestration``
30	extra, imported lazily).
31	"""
32	
33	from __future__ import annotations
34	
35	import warnings
36	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
37	
38	from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateError
39	
40	GateProposal = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], Mapping[str, Any]]
41	
42	_VALID_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved", "rejected", "deferred"})
43	
44	
45	def apply_gate_decision(gate: Gate, resumed: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
46	    """Validate a resumed operator decision and return the state update to record it.
47	
48	    Raises :class:`GateError` if the resume value is malformed, targets a different gate
49	    (correlation guard), or carries an unrecognized decision.
50	    """
51	    if not isinstance(resumed, Mapping):
52	        raise GateError(
53	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resume value must be a mapping, got {type(resumed).__name__}"
54	        )
55	    target = resumed.get("gate_id")
56	    if target != gate.name:
57	        raise GateError(
58	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resumed decision targets {target!r} — stale or misrouted; "
59	            "rejecting (gate-correlation guard)"
60	        )
61	    decision = str(resumed.get("decision", "")).strip().lower()
62	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
63	        raise GateError(
64	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: invalid decision {decision!r} "
65	            f"(expected one of {sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)})"
66	        )
67	    # A rejection/deferral with no rationale is governance-relevant; record a sentinel rather
68	    # than a blank (incl. whitespace-only) so the audit trail is never silently empty.
69	    rationale = str(resumed.get("rationale") or "").strip() or "No rationale provided."
70	    record = {"gate": gate.name, "decision": decision, "rationale": rationale}
71	    return {"gate_decisions": [record]}
72	
73	
74	# Only an approval is terminal: a re-entered approved gate is a no-op, but a rejected/deferred
75	# gate must re-fire its interrupt so a revise→re-review loop can re-prompt the operator.
76	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved"})
77	
78	
79	def already_decided(state: Mapping[str, Any], gate_name: str) -> bool:
80	    """True if ``gate_name`` already holds a terminal (approved) decision in ``gate_decisions``."""
81	    for record in state.get("gate_decisions", []) or []:
82	        if record.get("gate") == gate_name and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS:
83	            return True
84	    return False
85	
86	
87	GateRecorder = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None]
88	
89	
90	def make_gate_node(
91	    gate: Gate,
92	    propose: GateProposal,
93	    *,
94	    recorder: GateRecorder | None = None,
95	) -> Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], dict[str, Any]]:
96	    """Build a LangGraph node that parks at ``gate`` for an operator decision.
97	
98	    ``propose(state)`` returns the proposal payload surfaced to the operator (what the gate is
99	    asking them to approve). The node returns a ``{"gate_decisions": [record]}`` update, or
100	    ``{}`` (no-op) if the gate is already terminally approved.
101	
102	    ``recorder`` (optional) bridges each validated decision into the hash-chained provenance
103	    trail (#111): the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as
104	    (untamper-evident) graph state, so the documented append-only trail would miss them. Pass a
105	    callback such as ``lambda r: trail.record("gate", f"gate {r['gate']}: {r['decision']}", **r)``
106	    so the graph path's approvals/rejections land in the same chained trail as the runner's.
107	
108	    Omitting ``recorder`` is a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap: gate decisions then live only in
109	    (non-tamper-evident) LangGraph state, never in the hash-chained trail, so a checkpoint edit
110	    flipping ``rejected``→``approved`` is undetectable by ``verify_records``. Because that
111	    insecure configuration is effortless to ship by accident, building a node without a recorder
112	    emits a warning rather than failing silently (pass 3, #F-007).
113	    """
114	    if recorder is None:
115	        warnings.warn(
116	            f"make_gate_node({gate.name!r}) built with no recorder: gate decisions will NOT be "
117	            "written to the hash-chained provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap) — pass a "
118	            "recorder that calls trail.record('gate', ...) for a tamper-evident audit trail",
119	            stacklevel=2,
120	        )
121	
122	    def node(state: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
123	        if already_decided(state, gate.name):
124	            return {}  # terminal decision exists — do not re-prompt or re-record
125	        try:
126	            from langgraph.types import interrupt
127	        except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
128	            raise RuntimeError(
129	                "orchestration requires the 'orchestration' extra (langgraph) — not installed"
130	            ) from exc
131	        proposal = dict(propose(state))
132	        current: dict[str, Any] = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal}
133	        # Validate inside a loop: on a bad/mis-correlated resume, re-fire interrupt (next
134	        # resume index) carrying the error rather than raising — a raise would strand the
135	        # bad value in the checkpoint and replay it forever, bricking the gate.
136	        while True:
137	            resumed = interrupt(current)
138	            try:
139	                update = apply_gate_decision(gate, resumed)
140	            except GateError as exc:
141	                current = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal, "error": str(exc)}
142	                continue
143	            if recorder is not None:
144	                for rec in update.get("gate_decisions", []):
145	                    recorder(rec)  # land the decision in the hash-chained provenance trail
146	            return update
147	
148	    return node
149

content.text: The code matches the description. Now let me verify the key claims: (1) does `interrupt()` consume/persist before validation, (2) does a recorder exception propagate, and (3) is the "never strand" pattern actually used elsewhere as cited.

content.text: `record` does raise `ProvenanceError` on real modes (clock raises, etc.), confirming a recorder built around `trail.record` can throw. Let me check whether pipeline.py has the cited "never mask / never strand" resilient pattern, and confirm interrupt semantics.

That's not central to whether the bug exists, but it matters for the finding's credibility. Let me check where recorder/trail resilience patterns actually live and confirm the runner's pattern.

content.text: The "never strand/mask" pattern is actually in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py` (top-level, not orchestration). The finding's path line is slightly off but the pattern exists. Now let me look at the actual resilient pattern in the top-level pipeline.py around 738, and verify the interrupt semantics claim, plus check tests for gatenode.

content.content:
725	        ok = True
726	    except Exception as exc:
727	        # Record a terminal failure entry BEFORE re-raising so the abort reason is recoverable
728	        # from the (caller-owned) trail, not lost (#109).
729	        last_phase = visited[-1].name if visited else "start"
730	        try:
731	            trail.record(
732	                "run_failed",
733	                f"{type(exc).__name__} during {last_phase}",
734	                error_type=type(exc).__name__,
735	                message=str(exc)[:2000],
736	                last_phase=last_phase,
737	            )
738	        except Exception as inner:  # noqa: BLE001 — never mask the original failure
739	            # The run_failed entry could not be recorded (payload unserializable, clock raised,
740	            # trail lock contended). Don't silently swallow — emit a structured warning so the
741	            # failure reason is at least in the logs, even though it is absent from the trail
742	            # (pass 3, #F-030). The original exception is still re-raised below.
743	            _log.warning(
744	                "could not record run_failed provenance entry (%s: %s)",
745	                type(inner).__name__,
746	                inner,
747	            )
748	        raise
749	    finally:
750	        trace_ctx.close()
751	        if tracker is not None and run_id is not None:
752	            try:
753	                # a failed run must be distinguishable from a successful one in the store (#110)
754	                tracker.end_run(run_id, status="FINISHED" if ok else "FAILED")

content.text:
The "never mask / never strand" pattern at pipeline.py:738-747 exists exactly as referenced (just in top-level pipeline.py, not orchestration/pipeline.py — a minor path imprecision). The pattern is real and is the established convention in this codebase for "record-best-effort, never strand."

Now the core question: is the gatenode behavior a genuine production bug? Let me confirm the interrupt() semantics claim and look at the existing tests.

input.description: Inspect graph tests for gatenode interrupt/resume

content.content:

content.text:
There is no test covering a recorder that raises after a valid resume. The tests confirm the design intent (re-prompt-don't-raise on bad resume) but do NOT cover the recorder-failure-after-valid-resume path. This corroborates the gap.

Now let me confirm the LangGraph `interrupt` semantics claim — that the resume value is consumed/persisted before the node returns. This is the load-bearing technical premise. It's well-documented LangGraph behavior: when a node is resumed via `Command(resume=...)`, the interrupt call returns the value, and the graph re-executes the node from the start on the next invocation if the node doesn't complete. The resume value is consumed from the pending interrupt. If the node raises after `interrupt()` returns, the node did not complete successfully, so on the next run it re-executes from the top — but the resume value has already been popped.

Let me confirm the node's own docstring asserts this and verify the loop structure once more for the exact failure mode.

content.text:
I have enough to make a determination. Let me reason through the analysis.

**The technical premise is correct.** In LangGraph, `interrupt()` works by: on resume (`Command(resume=value)`), the *entire node re-executes from the top*; previously-executed `interrupt()` calls return their previously-supplied values from a saved resume map, and the *new* resume value is delivered to the next not-yet-resumed interrupt. Critically, when a node completes successfully, the consumed resume values are cleared from pending state. But the key subtlety here is the in-loop re-fire of `interrupt()`:

- First invocation: node hits `interrupt(current)` → run parks, control returns to operator.
- Operator resumes with valid `approved` → node re-executes from top, `interrupt()` returns the approved value, `apply_gate_decision` validates OK, then `recorder(rec)` raises.
- The exception propagates out of `node()`. The node did NOT complete. The graph step fails. The checkpoint at this point still reflects the resume having been consumed for that interrupt index, but the node's write (the `{"gate_decisions": [...]}` update) is never committed because the node raised.
- On the next replay/resume attempt, the node re-executes from the top. `already_decided` is False (the terminal record was never written). The node fires `interrupt()` again. But there is no new pending resume value to deliver unless the operator submits a fresh one — the prior approved value was attached to the now-failed step.

So the validated decision is lost and the operator must be re-prompted — exactly the "strand" hazard the module explicitly defends against for bad resumes, but left unguarded for recorder failures on good resumes.

**Is the recorder-raises scenario real?** Yes. `provenance.record()` raises `ProvenanceError` on clock failures, unserializable payloads, and the documented recorder is literally `lambda r: trail.record(...)`. The graph.py docstring and gatenode docstring both recommend exactly that recorder. So a `ProvenanceError` from `recorder()` is a realistic, non-speculative mode.

**Is it already handled?** No. Line 143-145 calls `recorder(rec)` with no try/except, inside the loop, after `interrupt()` consumed the resume. The established codebase convention (pipeline.py:738-747, "never mask / never strand") is NOT applied here. The inconsistency is real and self-evident from the module's own docstring.

**Severity assessment:** This is a real correctness/availability bug on the durable governance path, but its blast radius is bounded: it requires (a) a recorder configured (the recommended/secure config), and (b) the recorder to actually raise (transient provenance failure). The consequence is operator re-prompting / lost validated decision plus a possibly-bricked gate on the durable path — a governance-integrity and availability concern, not data corruption or a security bypass. It is recoverable by operator re-submission once the recorder transient clears, though if the recorder failure is persistent the gate is effectively bricked. I assess **medium**: genuine, reproducible-by-design, matches the module's own stated hazard model, but conditional on a recorder-side failure and operator-recoverable in the transient case.

The PoC is sound. The only inaccuracy in the finding is the cross-reference path: it cites `pipeline.py:738-747` implying `orchestration/pipeline.py`, but that pattern actually lives in the top-level `src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:738-747`. The pattern itself exists exactly as described, so this is a citation imprecision, not a substantive error.

input.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a true production-readiness problem in current code.

Code matches the finding exactly. In /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py, make_gate_node's node() runs a `while True` loop: line 137 `resumed = interrupt(current)`, line 139 validates via apply_gate_decision, then lines 143-145 call `recorder(rec)` with NO try/except, then line 146 returns. The recorder call sits after interrupt() has already consumed/persisted the operator's resume value for that interrupt index.

Technical premise is correct. LangGraph's interrupt/resume model re-executes the whole node from the top on resume; the supplied resume value is delivered to the matching interrupt and is tied to that graph step. If the node raises after interrupt() returns (recorder throwing), the node does not complete, its `{"gate_decisions": [...]}` write is never committed, and `already_decided` stays False. On the next replay the node re-fires interrupt() (line 137) and re-prompts — the prior validated decision is lost / the gate is stranded on the durable governance path. This is precisely the strand-and-replay hazard the module documents (docstring lines 15-20, inline 133-135) and deliberately guards against for BAD resumes (GateError -> re-fire interrupt, lines 140-142), but leaves unguarded for recorder failures on a GOOD resume.

The recorder-raises mode is real, not speculative. provenance.py raises ProvenanceError on clock failures (provenance.py:131), non-aware timestamps (138), serialization/normalization failures (143). Both gatenode.py:105 and graph.py:63-64 recommend the recorder be `lambda r: trail.record("gate", ...)`, so a ProvenanceError escaping recorder() is a realistic transient/contention mode.

Not already handled: lines 143-145 have no error handling, and the codebase's own established "never mask / never strand" convention (record-best-effort, structured warning, then proceed) is applied in src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:738-747 but NOT here — a self-evident inconsistency. No test covers a recorder that raises after a valid resume (tests in test_orchestration_graph.py cover bad-resume re-prompt and happy-path recording only), corroborating the gap.

Finding inaccuracy (minor, non-substantive): it cites "pipeline.py:738-747" implying orchestration/pipeline.py, which does not exist; the referenced never-mask/never-strand pattern actually lives in top-level src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:738-747. The pattern exists exactly as described, so this is only a path/citation imprecision.

Severity medium rather than high: genuine correctness/availability bug on the durable governance path, but (a) only triggers when a recorder is configured (the recommended secure config) AND the recorder actually raises, and (b) in the transient case it is operator-recoverable via re-submission; it becomes a hard gate-brick only if the recorder failure is persistent. No data corruption or security bypass. The suggested fix (normalize recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning mirroring pipeline.py:738-747) is appropriate.

content.text: Confirmed. Finding G-020 is a true production-readiness problem in current code at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:143-145`, severity medium.
